summary: Stancil argues that most dashboard-driven analysis is closer to roulette than science: we see patterns and invent narratives in essentially noisy data. He praises Cedric Chin’s simple, iterative method for reading operational data because it gives permission to ignore false complexity and focus on actionable variation. The post then pivots to influencer culture, framing brand value as a social association game where the “wrong” endorsers can erode or even perversely strengthen a brand.
money_quotes: Roulette is one giant dice roll.
The better answer, I think, is that Chin’s post gives us permission to make this simple.
When you investigate everything, you find nothing; when you find nothing, you doubt your methods; and when you doubt your methods, you flail.
Much better to get paid for the things you don’t do.
key_insight: Good analysis comes less from sophisticated techniques than from disciplined, simple observation of meaningful variation—and social perception games in branding operate by similarly irrational but powerful pattern‑seeking dynamics.
